  player_impact_level varchar [note: 'Severity of player-facing service degradation during the maintenance window: none (no player impact), low (minor latency), medium (feature unavailable), high (game unplayable), critical (complete service outage).']
  post_maintenance_validation_status varchar [note: 'Status of post-maintenance testing and validation: not_started (validation pending), in_progress (tests running), passed (all checks successful), failed (issues detected), skipped (validation not performed).']
  rollback_plan_available boolean [note: 'Indicates whether a tested rollback plan exists (True) to revert changes if maintenance fails. Required for high-impact maintenance windows.']
  scheduled_end_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Planned date and time when the maintenance window is scheduled to conclude. Used for SLA exclusion calculations and player communication.']
  scheduled_start_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Planned date and time when the maintenance window is scheduled to begin. Used for player notifications and capacity planning.']
  sla_exclusion_flag boolean [note: 'Indicates whether this maintenance window should be excluded from uptime SLA calculations (True) or counted as downtime (False). Planned maintenance is typically excluded.']
  window_code varchar [note: 'Externally-known unique code for the maintenance window, used in player communications and SLA tracking (e.g., \'MW-20240315001\').']
  window_name varchar [note: 'Human-readable name or title for the maintenance window (e.g., \'Q1 2024 Platform Upgrade\', \'Emergency CDN Patch\').']

  Note: 'Master record for planned maintenance windows during which live service infrastructure components are taken offline or degraded for patching, upgrades, or capacity changes. Captures window name, affected component type and references, scheduled start and end timestamps, actual start and end timestamps, maintenance type (patch, upgrade, migration, emergency), player-facing impact level, notification status, and approving engineer. Enables proactive player communication and SLA exclusion tracking.'
}

Table infrastructure.security_event {
  security_event_id varchar [pk, note: 'Unique identifier for the infrastructure security event record. Primary key.']
  cdn_node_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to infrastructure.cdn_node. Business justification: security_event can target a cdn_node. Many security events for one CDN node (N:1). security_event.target_service_component (STRING) can reference a CDN node. Adding cdn_node_id FK to link security eve']
  compliance_incident_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to compliance.compliance_incident. Business justification: Security events (DDoS, unauthorized access, data exfiltration) that affect player data or violate regulations become compliance incidents. Gaming companies track the compliance dimension of security e']
  correlated_security_event_id varchar [note: 'Self-referencing FK on security_event (correlated_security_event_id)']
  cost_center_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to finance.cost_center. Business justification: Security incident response and mitigation costs are tracked by cost center for financial reporting. Essential for security cost accounting and budget variance analysis.']
  defect_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to quality.defect. Business justification: Security vulnerabilities are tracked as defects in JIRA. Security incident response includes defect creation for vulnerability remediation tracking, compliance reporting (GDPR, PCI-DSS), and certifica']
  employee_id varchar [note: 'Reference to the security engineer or DevOps engineer assigned to investigate and respond to this security event.']
  game_server_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to infrastructure.game_server. Business justification: security_event can target a specific game_server. Many security events for one server (N:1). security_event.target_service_component (STRING) can reference a server. Adding game_server_id FK to link s']
  game_title_id varchar [note: 'Reference to the game title affected by this security event.']
  infrastructure_incident_id varchar [note: 'Reference to the escalated infrastructure incident if this security event triggered formal incident management response.']
  network_region_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to infrastructure.network_region. Business justification: security_event affects a network_region. Many security events in one region (N:1). security_event.affected_region_code (STRING) matches network_region.region_code. Adding network_region_id FK and remo']
  server_fleet_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to infrastructure.server_fleet. Business justification: security_event can target a server_fleet. Many security events for one fleet (N:1). security_event.target_service_component (STRING) can reference a fleet. Adding server_fleet_id FK to link security e']
  telemetry_pipeline_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to analytics.telemetry_pipeline. Business justification: Security events (DDoS attacks, exploit attempts) are ingested through dedicated telemetry pipelines for real-time threat detection and compliance reporting. Security teams track pipeline health for in']
  title_pl_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to finance.title_pl. Business justification: Security events impact title P&L through mitigation costs and potential revenue loss. Essential for security incident financial impact assessment and P&L variance explanations.']
  attack_vector varchar [note: 'Technical description of the method or pathway used to execute the security threat (e.g., volumetric flood, application layer exploit, authentication bypass).']
  automated_response_enabled boolean [note: 'Indicates whether automated mitigation actions were enabled and executed without manual intervention.']
  ccu_affected varchar [note: 'Estimated number of concurrent users impacted by the security event through service degradation or unavailability.']
  communication_sent_flag boolean [note: 'Indicates whether player-facing or stakeholder communication was sent regarding the security event and its impact.']
  created_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Date and time when this security event record was first created in the system. Format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX.']
  detection_source varchar [note: 'Origin of the security event detection indicating whether it was identified by automated monitoring, manual observation, external security service, or player report.']
  detection_system varchar [note: 'Name or identifier of the monitoring system, tool, or service that detected the security event (e.g., CloudFlare, AWS Shield, internal SIEM, WAF).']
  detection_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Date and time when the security event was first detected by automated monitoring or manual observation. Format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX.']
  escalated_flag boolean [note: 'Indicates whether the security event was escalated to formal infrastructure incident management due to severity or impact.']
  estimated_revenue_impact_usd decimal(15,2) [note: 'Estimated financial impact in US dollars due to service downtime, player churn, or lost monetization opportunities caused by the security event.']
  event_number varchar [note: 'Human-readable unique identifier for the security event, used for tracking and communication. Format: SE-YYYYMMDD-XXXXXX.']
  event_status varchar [note: 'Current lifecycle state of the security event in the response workflow.']
  event_type varchar [note: 'Classification of the security threat or attack vector detected. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: ddos_attack|brute_force|credential_stuffing|waf_trigger|rate_limit_breach|bot_detection|exploit_attempt|sql_injection|xss_attack|unauthorized_access|data_exfiltration|malware_detection — promote to reference product]']
  false_positive_flag boolean [note: 'Indicates whether the security event was later determined to be a false positive after investigation.']
  latency_spike_ms decimal(10,2) [note: 'Maximum increase in network latency in milliseconds observed during the security event, indicating player experience degradation.']
  mitigation_action varchar [note: 'Primary defensive action taken to mitigate the security threat.']
  mitigation_description varchar [note: 'Detailed narrative of the mitigation steps taken, including configuration changes, rules applied, and response actions executed.']
  mitigation_end_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Date and time when mitigation actions were completed and the threat was neutralized. Format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX.']
  mitigation_start_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Date and time when mitigation actions were initiated to counter the security threat. Format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX.']
  modified_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Date and time when this security event record was last updated. Format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX.']
  notes varchar [note: 'Free-form text field for additional context, observations, or operational notes related to the security event.']
  peak_attack_volume_gbps decimal(12,3) [note: 'Maximum network traffic volume in gigabits per second observed during the attack, applicable to volumetric DDoS events.']
  peak_attack_volume_mpps decimal(12,3) [note: 'Maximum packet rate in millions of packets per second observed during the attack, applicable to protocol-based DDoS events.']
  player_impact_assessment varchar [note: 'Qualitative assessment of the impact on player experience and game service availability.']
  post_mortem_reference varchar [note: 'Reference identifier or URL to the detailed post-mortem analysis document or report for this security event.']
  remediation_action_items varchar [note: 'List of follow-up actions, patches, or configuration changes required to prevent recurrence of similar security events.']
  request_count varchar [note: 'Total number of malicious or suspicious requests detected during the security event (e.g., login attempts, API calls, bot requests).']
  resolution_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Date and time when the security event was fully resolved and services restored to normal operation. Format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX.']
  root_cause_category varchar [note: 'High-level classification of the underlying cause or vulnerability exploited in the security event (e.g., configuration weakness, unpatched vulnerability, credential compromise).']
  root_cause_description varchar [note: 'Detailed narrative explanation of the root cause analysis findings and contributing factors to the security event.']
  service_downtime_minutes decimal(10,2) [note: 'Total duration in minutes that affected services were unavailable or degraded due to the security event.']
  severity_level varchar [note: 'Business impact severity classification of the security event based on threat level and player impact.']
  sla_breach_flag boolean [note: 'Indicates whether the security event caused a breach of uptime, latency, or availability SLA commitments.']
  source_country_code varchar [note: 'Three-letter ISO country code of the geographic origin of the attack based on IP geolocation.']
  source_ip_address varchar [note: 'Originating IP address or CIDR block from which the security threat originated. May be single IP or range.']
  tags_metadata varchar [note: 'Flexible JSON or comma-separated key-value pairs for additional categorization, labeling, or operational metadata (e.g., campaign tags, environment labels).']
  target_service_component varchar [note: 'Specific infrastructure service component or endpoint that was targeted by the security event (e.g., matchmaking API, game server cluster, CDN edge, authentication service).']

  Note: 'Transactional record for infrastructure security incidents and threat events affecting live game services. Captures event identifier, event type (DDoS attack, brute force, credential stuffing, WAF trigger, rate limit breach, bot detection, exploit attempt), source IP/CIDR, target service component, affected region, attack vector, peak attack volume (Gbps/Mpps), mitigation action taken (blackhole, rate limit, CDN shield, geo-block), mitigation start and end timestamps, player impact assessment (CCU affected, latency spike), associated incident reference if escalated, and detection source (automated/manual). SSOT for all infrastructure security threat tracking, enabling DDoS response playbooks, attack pattern analysis, and security posture reporting for GaaS live services.'
}

Table infrastructure.infrastructure_test_execution {
  infrastructure_test_execution_id varchar [pk, note: 'Primary key for infrastructure_test_execution']
  game_server_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to the game server instance on which this test case was executed']
  test_case_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to the test case that was executed in this test run']
  test_execution_id varchar [note: 'Unique identifier for this test execution record. Primary key.']
  build_version varchar [note: 'The specific game build version that was under test during this execution. Explicitly identified in detection phase relationship data.']
  executed_by varchar [note: 'Name or identifier of the QA engineer or automated system that initiated this test execution.']
  execution_duration_seconds varchar [note: 'Duration in seconds that this test execution took to complete. Explicitly identified in detection phase relationship data.']
  execution_status varchar [note: 'Result status of this test execution. Explicitly identified in detection phase relationship data. Values: PASSED, FAILED, BLOCKED, SKIPPED, IN_PROGRESS.']
  execution_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Timestamp when this test case execution was initiated on the game server. Explicitly identified in detection phase relationship data.']
  failure_reason varchar [note: 'Detailed description of why the test failed, if execution_status is FAILED. Null for passed tests.']
  jira_test_run_key varchar [note: 'Reference to the Jira test run or test execution ticket that tracks this execution in the external system.']
  test_environment varchar [note: 'The environment context in which this test was executed (e.g., Dev, QA, Staging, Production-like). Explicitly identified in detection phase relationship data.']

  Note: 'This association product represents the execution event between game_server and test_case. It captures the operational record of running a specific test case against a specific game server instance. Each record links one game_server to one test_case with execution-specific attributes that exist only in the context of this test run.. Existence Justification: In gaming QA operations, test cases are executed against multiple game server instances (different regions, configurations, OS versions, build versions) to validate functionality across the fleet. Each game server hosts executions of multiple test cases as part of regression, performance, and certification testing. The business actively manages test execution records as operational QA artifacts.'
}

Table infrastructure.monitoring_alert {
  monitoring_alert_id varchar [pk, note: 'Primary key for monitoring_alert']
  infrastructure_incident_id varchar [note: 'The external incident management system ticket identifier (e.g., Jira, ServiceNow) created for this alert if it required formal incident tracking.']
  parent_monitoring_alert_id varchar [note: 'Self-referencing FK on monitoring_alert (parent_monitoring_alert_id)']
  suppression_rule_id varchar [note: 'The identifier of the alert suppression or maintenance window rule that muted this alert, if applicable. Used during planned maintenance or known issue periods.']
  acknowledged_timestamp timestamp [note: 'The date and time when the alert was acknowledged by an on-call engineer or automated system, indicating response has begun.']
  affected_player_count varchar [note: 'The estimated number of concurrent players or user sessions impacted by the alert condition at the time of trigger. Critical for assessing business impact and prioritization.']
  alert_code varchar [note: 'Externally-known unique code identifying the alert type and category. Format: three-letter category prefix followed by four-digit sequence number (e.g., SRV-0001, NET-0042).']
  alert_description varchar [note: 'Detailed description of the alert condition, including business impact, affected services, and recommended actions for responders.']
  alert_message varchar [note: 'The specific alert message generated at trigger time, including dynamic values and context (e.g., \'CPU utilization 95% on server game-srv-042 exceeds threshold 80%\').']
  alert_name varchar [note: 'Human-readable name of the monitoring alert, describing the condition being monitored.']
  alert_type varchar [note: 'Classification of the alert based on the monitoring condition: threshold (metric exceeds limit), anomaly (unusual pattern detected), availability (service up/down), performance (latency/throughput degradation), security (threat detected), capacity (resource exhaustion).']
  assigned_engineer varchar [note: 'The name or identifier of the specific engineer or responder currently assigned to investigate and resolve the alert.']
  assigned_team varchar [note: 'The name of the engineering or operations team assigned to respond to the alert (e.g., game-server-ops, network-engineering, platform-reliability, security-response).']
  auto_resolved_flag boolean [note: 'Boolean indicator of whether the alert was automatically resolved by the monitoring system (true) or required manual intervention (false). Used to measure self-healing infrastructure effectiveness.']
  availability_zone varchar [note: 'The cloud provider availability zone or data center identifier where the resource is deployed. Used for fault domain analysis and capacity planning.']
  closed_timestamp timestamp [note: 'The date and time when the alert incident was formally closed after verification and post-incident review.']
  created_timestamp timestamp [note: 'The date and time when this monitoring alert record was first created in the data platform. Audit trail for data lineage.']
  environment varchar [note: 'The deployment environment where the monitored resource is running: production (live player-facing), staging (pre-production testing), development (active development), qa (quality assurance), disaster_recovery (failover standby).']
  escalation_level varchar [note: 'The current escalation tier for the alert (0 = initial on-call team, 1 = senior engineer, 2 = engineering manager, 3 = director/VP, 4 = executive). Increments based on time-to-acknowledge and severity.']
  evaluation_window_seconds varchar [note: 'The time window in seconds over which the metric is evaluated before triggering the alert. Prevents false positives from transient spikes.']
  game_title varchar [note: 'The specific game title or franchise affected by the alert. Critical for prioritizing alerts based on player base and revenue impact.']
  metric_name varchar [note: 'The name of the performance or health metric being monitored (e.g., cpu_utilization_percent, network_latency_ms, active_player_count, server_response_time_ms).']
  metric_unit varchar [note: 'The unit of measurement for the metric value (e.g., percent, milliseconds, requests_per_second, concurrent_users, gigabytes).']
  metric_value decimal(18,4) [note: 'The observed value of the monitored metric at the time the alert was triggered. This is the principal quantitative fact that caused the alert condition.']
  notification_channels varchar [note: 'Comma-separated list of notification channels used to alert responders (e.g., pagerduty, slack, email, sms, webhook). Tracks how the alert was communicated.']
  platform varchar [note: 'The gaming platform affected by the alert: pc (Windows/Mac/Linux), console (PlayStation/Xbox/Nintendo), mobile (iOS/Android), cross_platform (multi-platform service).']
  priority varchar [note: 'Operational priority for alert response: P1 (immediate response required), P2 (urgent), P3 (normal), P4 (low), P5 (planning). Derived from severity and business impact.']
  region varchar [note: 'Three-letter geographic region code where the monitored resource is deployed (e.g., USE for US East, EUW for EU West, APS for Asia Pacific South). Critical for regional SLA tracking and player experience monitoring.']
  resolution_notes varchar [note: 'Detailed notes documenting the investigation process, root cause findings, remediation actions taken, and any follow-up tasks. Populated after alert resolution for knowledge management.']
  resolved_timestamp timestamp [note: 'The date and time when the underlying condition was resolved and the alert was cleared.']
  resource_identifier varchar [note: 'The unique identifier of the specific resource instance being monitored (e.g., server hostname, instance ID, service endpoint, IP address).']
  resource_type varchar [note: 'The type of infrastructure resource being monitored: game_server (dedicated or cloud game server instance), matchmaking_service (player matchmaking backend), cdn_node (content delivery network edge node), database (persistent data store), load_balancer (traffic distribution), network_device (router/switch), storage_volume (disk/SAN).']
  root_cause_category varchar [note: 'The high-level category of the identified root cause after investigation (e.g., hardware_failure, software_bug, capacity_limit, network_issue, configuration_error, third_party_dependency, ddos_attack). Populated after resolution for trend analysis. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: hardware_failure|software_bug|capacity_limit|network_issue|configuration_error|third_party_dependency|ddos_attack|player_surge|deployment_issue|database_contention — promote to reference product]']
  service_name varchar [note: 'The name of the game service or application component affected by the alert (e.g., player-authentication, game-session-manager, leaderboard-api, voice-chat-service).']
  severity varchar [note: 'Business impact severity of the alert: critical (service outage, immediate action required), high (major degradation), medium (minor impact), low (potential issue), informational (FYI only).']
  sla_breach_flag boolean [note: 'Boolean indicator of whether this alert represents a breach of defined service level agreement thresholds (e.g., uptime SLA, latency SLA, availability SLA). True indicates SLA breach.']
  source_system varchar [note: 'The monitoring or observability system that generated the alert (e.g., Prometheus, Datadog, New Relic, CloudWatch, Grafana).']
  status varchar [note: 'Current lifecycle state of the alert: active (triggered, awaiting acknowledgment), acknowledged (team notified), investigating (root cause analysis in progress), resolved (condition cleared), closed (incident closed), suppressed (muted by rule).']
  threshold_operator varchar [note: 'The comparison operator used to evaluate the metric against the threshold (e.g., greater_than for high-water marks, less_than for low-water marks).']
  threshold_value decimal(18,4) [note: 'The configured threshold value that, when exceeded or breached, triggers the alert. Used for threshold-type alerts.']
  triggered_timestamp timestamp [note: 'The exact date and time when the monitoring condition was first detected and the alert was triggered. This is the principal business event timestamp for the alert lifecycle.']
  updated_timestamp timestamp [note: 'The date and time when this monitoring alert record was last modified. Tracks changes to alert status, assignments, and resolution details.']

  Note: 'Master reference table for monitoring_alert. Referenced by monitoring_alert_id.'
}

Table infrastructure.change_request {
  change_request_id varchar [pk, note: 'Primary key for change_request']
  approved_by_user_employee_id varchar [note: 'Identifier of the user or authority who approved the change request for implementation.']
  assigned_to_user_employee_id varchar [note: 'Identifier of the user or team member responsible for implementing the change request.']
  configuration_item_id varchar [note: 'Identifier of the primary configuration item or infrastructure component being modified by this change.']
  employee_id varchar [note: 'Identifier of the user or team member who initiated the change request.']
  org_unit_id varchar [note: 'Identifier of the technical team responsible for executing the change request.']
  related_incident_infrastructure_incident_id varchar [note: 'Identifier of the incident that triggered this change request, if the change is remedial in nature.']
  related_problem_id varchar [note: 'Identifier of the problem record that this change request addresses as a permanent fix.']
  rollback_change_request_id varchar [note: 'Self-referencing FK on change_request (rollback_change_request_id)']
  actual_downtime_minutes varchar [note: 'Actual duration of service downtime or degradation that occurred during change implementation, measured in minutes.']
  actual_end_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Actual timestamp when change implementation was completed.']
  actual_start_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Actual timestamp when change implementation began.']
  affected_regions varchar [note: 'Geographic regions or data center locations where the change will be deployed, relevant for global game infrastructure.']
  affected_services varchar [note: 'List of game services, platforms, or infrastructure components that will be impacted by the change implementation.']
  approved_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Timestamp when the change request received formal approval for implementation.']
  cab_approval_required boolean [note: 'Indicates whether the change requires formal review and approval by the Change Advisory Board based on risk and impact.']
  cancelled_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Timestamp when the change request was cancelled before implementation.']
  change_category varchar [note: 'Functional area or domain affected by the change request, used for routing to appropriate technical teams and impact analysis.']
  change_type varchar [note: 'Classification of the change request based on urgency, risk, and approval requirements. Standard changes are pre-approved low-risk changes; normal changes require CAB approval; emergency changes address critical incidents; expedited changes are fast-tracked for business-critical needs.']
  completed_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Timestamp when the change request was formally closed and marked as completed, including post-implementation review.']
  created_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Timestamp when this change request record was first created in the system.']
  description varchar [note: 'Detailed description of the change request including technical details, business justification, and expected outcomes.']
  estimated_downtime_minutes varchar [note: 'Expected duration of service downtime or degradation during change implementation, measured in minutes.']
  impact_assessment varchar [note: 'Analysis of potential impact on game services, infrastructure components, player experience, and business operations if the change is implemented.']
  implementation_notes varchar [note: 'Technical notes and observations recorded during change implementation, including any deviations from the plan.']
  implementation_successful boolean [note: 'Indicates whether the change implementation met success criteria without requiring rollback.']
  justification varchar [note: 'Business rationale and expected benefits for implementing the change, including impact on player experience, operational efficiency, or revenue.']
  maintenance_window_required boolean [note: 'Indicates whether the change requires a scheduled maintenance window with player notification and service downtime.']
  player_notification_required boolean [note: 'Indicates whether advance notification to players is required due to expected service impact or downtime.']
  post_implementation_review varchar [note: 'Assessment of change success, lessons learned, and recommendations for future similar changes.']
  priority varchar [note: 'Business priority level assigned to the change request, determining scheduling and resource allocation.']
  request_number varchar [note: 'Human-readable business identifier for the change request, typically following a standardized format for external communication and tracking.']
  requested_date date [note: 'Date when the change request was initially submitted.']
  requested_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Precise timestamp when the change request was submitted, representing the principal business event time for this transaction.']
  risk_level varchar [note: 'Assessed risk level of implementing the change, considering potential impact on service availability, player experience, and business operations.']
  rollback_executed boolean [note: 'Indicates whether the rollback plan was executed due to implementation failure or unexpected issues.']
  rollback_plan varchar [note: 'Documented procedure for reverting the change if implementation fails or causes unexpected issues, ensuring service continuity.']
  scheduled_end_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Planned timestamp for completing the change implementation.']
  scheduled_start_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Planned timestamp for beginning the change implementation.']
  status varchar [note: 'Current lifecycle state of the change request in the approval and implementation workflow.']
  success_criteria varchar [note: 'Measurable criteria that define successful completion of the change, used for post-implementation validation.']
  title varchar [note: 'Brief descriptive title summarizing the purpose and scope of the change request.']
  updated_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Timestamp when this change request record was last modified.']

  Note: 'Master reference table for change_request. Referenced by change_request_id.'
}

Table infrastructure.matchmaking_service {
  matchmaking_service_id varchar [pk, note: 'Primary key for matchmaking_service']
  fallback_matchmaking_service_id varchar [note: 'Self-referencing FK on matchmaking_service (fallback_matchmaking_service_id)']
  server_fleet_id varchar [note: 'Reference to the server pool or fleet that hosts matches created by this matchmaking service.']
  algorithm_type varchar [note: 'The matchmaking algorithm used to pair players (skill-based matchmaking, connection-based, hybrid, Elo rating, Glicko, TrueSkill, etc.).']
  api_endpoint_url varchar [note: 'URL of the API endpoint that clients use to interact with this matchmaking service.']
  auto_scaling_enabled boolean [note: 'Indicates whether this matchmaking service has auto-scaling enabled to dynamically adjust capacity based on demand.']
  avg_match_time_seconds varchar [note: 'Average time in seconds for a typical match session facilitated by this matchmaking service.']
  avg_queue_time_seconds varchar [note: 'Average time in seconds players spend waiting in the matchmaking queue before being matched.']
  backfill_enabled boolean [note: 'Indicates whether this matchmaking service supports backfilling players into in-progress matches when slots become available.']
  ccu_capacity varchar [note: 'Maximum number of concurrent users this matchmaking service is provisioned to handle simultaneously.']
  cdn_endpoint_url varchar [note: 'URL of the CDN endpoint used to serve matchmaking service configuration and assets to clients.']
  created_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Timestamp when this matchmaking service record was first created in the system.']
  cross_platform_enabled boolean [note: 'Indicates whether this matchmaking service allows players from different platforms (console, PC, mobile) to be matched together.']
  deployment_environment varchar [note: 'The deployment environment where this matchmaking service instance is running.']
  deprecation_reason varchar [note: 'Business justification or technical reason for deprecating this matchmaking service, if applicable.']
  description varchar [note: 'Detailed description of the matchmaking service purpose, features, and operational characteristics.']
  effective_end_date date [note: 'Date when this matchmaking service was or will be deactivated or deprecated. Null for currently active services.']
  effective_start_date date [note: 'Date when this matchmaking service became active and available to players.']
  game_mode varchar [note: 'The primary game mode supported by this matchmaking service (Player vs Player, Player vs Environment, Massively Multiplayer Online, etc.).']
  health_check_interval_seconds varchar [note: 'Frequency in seconds at which the matchmaking service health status is monitored and reported.']
  incident_count_last_30_days varchar [note: 'Number of incidents reported for this matchmaking service in the last 30 days.']
  is_deprecated boolean [note: 'Indicates whether this matchmaking service has been deprecated and is scheduled for retirement.']
  last_deployment_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Timestamp when this matchmaking service version was last deployed to the environment.']
  latency_threshold_ms varchar [note: 'Maximum acceptable network latency in milliseconds for players matched through this service to ensure quality gameplay experience.']
  max_party_size varchar [note: 'Maximum number of players allowed in a single party when entering this matchmaking service queue.']
  max_players varchar [note: 'Maximum number of players allowed in a match created by this service.']
  max_queue_time_seconds varchar [note: 'Maximum allowed queue time in seconds before the matchmaking service relaxes matching criteria or times out.']
  min_players varchar [note: 'Minimum number of players required to start a match through this service.']
  modified_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Timestamp when this matchmaking service record was last modified or updated.']
  owner_team varchar [note: 'Name of the engineering or operations team responsible for maintaining and supporting this matchmaking service.']
  party_support_enabled boolean [note: 'Indicates whether this matchmaking service supports pre-formed player parties or groups entering the queue together.']
  pcu_peak_capacity varchar [note: 'Peak concurrent user capacity this matchmaking service can scale to during high-demand periods.']
  priority_weight decimal(5,2) [note: 'Weighting factor used to prioritize this matchmaking service in resource allocation and queue processing (higher values = higher priority).']
  rank_tier_max varchar [note: 'Maximum player rank or tier allowed in this matchmaking service to maintain competitive balance.']
  rank_tier_min varchar [note: 'Minimum player rank or tier required to access this matchmaking service (e.g., \'Bronze\', \'Silver\', \'Gold\').']
  region varchar [note: 'Three-letter ISO region code where this matchmaking service operates (e.g., USA, EUR, APC for Asia-Pacific).']
  service_code varchar [note: 'Unique business identifier code for the matchmaking service used in operational systems and APIs.']
  service_name varchar [note: 'Human-readable name of the matchmaking service (e.g., \'Ranked PvP Matchmaker\', \'Casual MMO Queue\', \'Battle Royale Lobby Service\').']
  service_type varchar [note: 'Classification of the matchmaking service by gameplay mode and competitive structure.']
  skill_range_tolerance decimal(5,2) [note: 'Percentage tolerance for skill rating variance allowed when matching players (e.g., 10.00 means +/- 10% skill rating).']
  status varchar [note: 'Current operational lifecycle status of the matchmaking service.']
  uptime_sla_percentage decimal(5,2) [note: 'Target uptime percentage defined in the service level agreement for this matchmaking service (e.g., 99.95).']
  version varchar [note: 'Semantic version number of the matchmaking service software (e.g., 2.5.1).']

  Note: 'Master reference table for matchmaking_service. Referenced by matchmaking_service_id.'
}

Table infrastructure.problem {
  problem_id varchar [pk, note: 'Primary key for problem']
  affected_platform varchar [note: 'Gaming platform affected by the problem. Indicates whether issue is platform-specific or cross-platform.']
  affected_region varchar [note: 'Geographic region or data center location affected by the problem. Uses 3-letter region codes (e.g., USE, EUW, APS).']
  affected_service varchar [note: 'Name of the game service or infrastructure component affected by this problem. Examples: matchmaking, authentication, CDN, game servers.']
  assigned_engineer varchar [note: 'Name or identifier of the engineer currently assigned to investigate and resolve the problem.']
  assigned_team varchar [note: 'Engineering team or group responsible for investigating and resolving the problem. Examples: DevOps, Network Engineering, Platform Engineering.']
  change_request_id varchar [note: 'Identifier of the change request created to implement the permanent solution for this problem. Links problem to change management process.']
  closed_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Date and time when the problem record was formally closed after verification. Final lifecycle timestamp for the problem.']
  created_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Date and time when this problem record was created in the system. Audit field for record lifecycle tracking.']
  description varchar [note: 'Detailed description of the problem including symptoms, impact, and context. Provides comprehensive information for root cause analysis.']
  detected_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Date and time when the problem was first detected or identified. Used for timeline analysis and response time measurement.']
  detection_method varchar [note: 'Method by which the problem was detected. Used to measure effectiveness of monitoring and proactive problem management.']
  downtime_minutes varchar [note: 'Total service downtime in minutes caused by this problem. Used for SLA tracking and availability reporting.']
  incident_count varchar [note: 'Number of incidents linked to this problem. Used to measure problem impact and prioritize resolution efforts.']
  investigation_started_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Date and time when formal investigation of the problem began. Used to track time-to-investigation metrics.']
  is_known_error boolean [note: 'Flag indicating whether this problem has been classified as a known error with documented workaround. True when root cause is identified but permanent fix is pending.']
  is_recurring boolean [note: 'Flag indicating whether this is a recurring problem that has occurred multiple times. Used to identify systemic issues requiring architectural changes.']
  permanent_solution varchar [note: 'Description of the permanent fix or solution implemented to resolve the problem. Documents the final resolution approach.']
  player_impact_count varchar [note: 'Estimated number of players affected by this problem. Critical metric for prioritization and business impact assessment.']
  priority varchar [note: 'Priority level for problem resolution based on severity and business impact. P1 is highest priority requiring immediate action.']
  problem_number varchar [note: 'Human-readable unique problem ticket number used for external reference and tracking. Format: PRB-YYYYMMDD.']
  problem_type varchar [note: 'Classification of the problem by technical domain. Used for routing to appropriate engineering teams and trend analysis.']
  related_problem_id varchar [note: 'Reference to another problem record that is related or linked to this problem. Enables tracking of problem dependencies and patterns.']
  resolved_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Date and time when the problem was resolved with a permanent solution. Used for resolution time tracking and SLA compliance.']
  root_cause varchar [note: 'Identified root cause of the problem after investigation. Documents the underlying technical or process issue causing incidents.']
  root_cause_identified_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Date and time when the root cause was successfully identified. Used to measure investigation effectiveness and time-to-diagnosis.']
  severity varchar [note: 'Severity level indicating the business impact and urgency of the problem. Critical problems affect core game services and require immediate attention.']
  status varchar [note: 'Current lifecycle status of the problem record. Tracks progression from identification through resolution and closure.']
  title varchar [note: 'Short descriptive title summarizing the problem for quick identification and reporting.']
  updated_by varchar [note: 'Username or identifier of the user who last updated this problem record. Audit field for change accountability.']
  updated_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Date and time when this problem record was last modified. Audit field for change tracking and data freshness.']
  workaround varchar [note: 'Temporary workaround or mitigation steps to reduce impact while permanent fix is developed. Used for known error management.']
  created_by varchar [note: 'Username or identifier of the user who created this problem record. Audit field for accountability and traceability.']

  Note: 'Master reference table for problem. Referenced by related_problem_id.'
}

Table infrastructure.configuration_item {
  configuration_item_id varchar [pk, note: 'Primary key for configuration_item']
  parent_configuration_item_id varchar [note: 'Self-referencing FK on configuration_item (parent_configuration_item_id)']
  activated_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Timestamp when the configuration item was activated and entered operational service.']
  auto_scaling_enabled boolean [note: 'Indicates whether automatic scaling is enabled for this configuration item to dynamically adjust capacity based on demand.']
  availability_zone varchar [note: 'Specific availability zone or fault domain within the region where the configuration item is deployed.']
  backup_enabled boolean [note: 'Indicates whether automated backup is enabled for this configuration item to ensure data recovery capability.']
  bandwidth_mbps decimal(10,2) [note: 'Network bandwidth capacity allocated to the configuration item, measured in megabits per second.']
  capacity_ccu varchar [note: 'Maximum number of concurrent users (CCU) that the configuration item can support simultaneously.']
  capacity_pcu varchar [note: 'Peak concurrent users (PCU) capacity threshold for the configuration item, representing maximum load handling capability.']
  ci_category varchar [note: 'High-level category grouping for the configuration item (hardware, software, network, service, virtual, cloud).']
  ci_code varchar [note: 'Externally-known unique code or identifier for the configuration item, used for cross-system reference and asset tracking.']
  ci_name varchar [note: 'Human-readable name or label for the configuration item, used for identification and display purposes.']
  ci_status varchar [note: 'Current lifecycle status of the configuration item indicating its operational state (active, inactive, maintenance, decommissioned, provisioning, failed).']
  ci_type varchar [note: 'Classification of the configuration item by its functional role in the infrastructure (e.g., game server, matchmaking service, CDN node, load balancer, database instance, cache cluster, message queue). [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: game_server|matchmaking_service|cdn_node|load_balancer|database_instance|cache_cluster|message_queue|api_gateway|authentication_service|storage_service|monitoring_agent|backup_service|firewall|network_switch|container_orchestrator — promote to reference product]']
  compliance_required boolean [note: 'Indicates whether this configuration item is subject to regulatory compliance requirements and auditing.']
  cost_per_hour_usd decimal(10,4) [note: 'Hourly operational cost for running this configuration item, measured in US dollars.']
  cpu_cores varchar [note: 'Number of CPU cores allocated to the configuration item for compute processing.']
  created_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Timestamp when this configuration item record was first created in the system.']
  decommissioned_date date [note: 'Date when the configuration item was decommissioned and removed from active service.']
  deployment_method varchar [note: 'Method used to deploy the configuration item (manual, automated, CI/CD pipeline, container orchestration, serverless).']
  description varchar [note: 'Detailed textual description of the configuration item, its purpose, and its role in the infrastructure.']
  environment varchar [note: 'Deployment environment where the configuration item operates (production, staging, development, testing, disaster recovery, sandbox).']
  high_availability_enabled boolean [note: 'Indicates whether high availability configuration is enabled for this configuration item to ensure redundancy and failover capability.']
  hostname varchar [note: 'Network hostname or fully qualified domain name (FQDN) assigned to the configuration item.']
  instance_type varchar [note: 'Cloud provider instance type or hardware specification model for the configuration item (e.g., m5.xlarge, c5.2xlarge).']
  ip_address varchar [note: 'Primary IP address assigned to the configuration item for network communication.']
  last_maintenance_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Timestamp of the most recent maintenance activity performed on the configuration item.']
  memory_gb decimal(10,2) [note: 'Amount of memory (RAM) allocated to the configuration item, measured in gigabytes.']
  modified_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Timestamp when this configuration item record was last modified or updated.']
  monitoring_enabled boolean [note: 'Indicates whether active monitoring is enabled for this configuration item to track performance and health metrics.']
  next_maintenance_date date [note: 'Date when the next scheduled maintenance activity is planned for the configuration item.']
  operating_system varchar [note: 'Operating system running on the configuration item (e.g., Linux Ubuntu 22.04, Windows Server 2022).']
  owner_team varchar [note: 'Name of the team or organizational unit responsible for managing and maintaining this configuration item.']
  patch_level varchar [note: 'Current patch level or update version applied to the configuration item for security and maintenance.']
  port_number varchar [note: 'Primary network port number on which the configuration item listens for connections.']
  provisioned_date date [note: 'Date when the configuration item was initially provisioned and made available for use.']
  region varchar [note: 'Geographic region or data center location where the configuration item is deployed (e.g., us-east-1, eu-west-2, ap-southeast-1).']
  sla_fps_target varchar [note: 'Target frames per second (FPS) performance metric defined in the service level agreement for game server configuration items.']
  sla_latency_target_ms varchar [note: 'Target maximum latency in milliseconds defined in the service level agreement for this configuration item.']
  sla_uptime_target_percent decimal(5,2) [note: 'Target uptime percentage defined in the service level agreement for this configuration item (e.g., 99.95%).']
  software_version varchar [note: 'Version number of the primary software or application running on the configuration item.']
  storage_gb decimal(12,2) [note: 'Amount of persistent storage allocated to the configuration item, measured in gigabytes.']
  support_tier varchar [note: 'Support tier level assigned to the configuration item indicating priority and escalation path (tier 1, tier 2, tier 3, critical, standard).']
  vendor_name varchar [note: 'Name of the vendor or cloud provider supplying the configuration item (e.g., AWS, Azure, GCP, on-premise).']

  Note: 'Master reference table for configuration_item. Referenced by configuration_item_id.'
}

Table infrastructure.alert_suppression_rule {
  alert_suppression_rule_id varchar [pk, note: 'Primary key for alert_suppression_rule']
  superseded_alert_suppression_rule_id varchar [note: 'Self-referencing FK on alert_suppression_rule (superseded_alert_suppression_rule_id)']
  activation_count varchar [note: 'Number of times this suppression rule has been triggered and activated since creation.']
  alert_category varchar [note: 'Category of alerts targeted by this suppression rule, such as performance, availability, security, or capacity alerts.']
  alert_severity_filter varchar [note: 'Minimum severity level of alerts that this suppression rule will affect. Alerts below this severity are not suppressed.']
  approved_by_user_id varchar [note: 'Identifier of the user who approved this suppression rule for activation, if approval workflow is required.']
  approved_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Date and time when this suppression rule was approved for activation.']
  audit_trail_flag boolean [note: 'Indicates whether suppressed alerts are logged for audit and compliance purposes even when not displayed.']
  auto_expire_flag boolean [note: 'Indicates whether the suppression rule automatically expires after its effective end timestamp or requires manual deactivation.']
  business_justification varchar [note: 'Business rationale and approval context for creating this suppression rule, including incident or change request references.']
  condition_expression varchar [note: 'Logical expression or criteria that must be met for the suppression rule to activate and suppress matching alerts.']
  created_by_user_id varchar [note: 'Identifier of the user or system account that created this suppression rule.']
  created_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Date and time when this suppression rule record was first created in the system.']
  description varchar [note: 'Detailed description of the suppression rule purpose, business justification, and operational context.']
  effective_end_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Date and time when this suppression rule expires and stops suppressing alerts. Null indicates indefinite duration.']
  effective_start_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Date and time when this suppression rule becomes active and begins suppressing alerts.']
  escalation_bypass_flag boolean [note: 'Indicates whether suppressed alerts bypass normal escalation workflows and incident management processes.']
  incident_reference_id varchar [note: 'Reference identifier to the incident or change request that prompted the creation of this suppression rule.']
  last_activation_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Date and time when this suppression rule was most recently triggered and activated.']
  maintenance_window_id varchar [note: 'Reference to the planned maintenance window associated with this suppression rule, if applicable.']
  modified_by_user_id varchar [note: 'Identifier of the user or system account that last modified this suppression rule.']
  modified_timestamp timestamp [note: 'Date and time when this suppression rule record was last modified.']
  notification_override_flag boolean [note: 'Indicates whether this suppression rule also prevents notification delivery or only suppresses alert visibility.']
  priority_level varchar [note: 'Priority classification of the suppression rule determining its precedence when multiple rules apply to the same alert.']
  recurrence_pattern varchar [note: 'Cron expression or schedule pattern defining recurring time windows when the suppression rule is active.']
  rule_code varchar [note: 'Unique business identifier code for the suppression rule, used for external references and integrations.']
  rule_name varchar [note: 'Human-readable name of the alert suppression rule for identification and management purposes.']
  rule_type varchar [note: 'Classification of the suppression rule based on its triggering mechanism and operational purpose.']
  scope varchar [note: 'Defines the infrastructure scope to which this suppression rule applies, from global to individual server level.']
  status varchar [note: 'Current lifecycle status of the alert suppression rule indicating whether it is operational and enforced.']
  suppressed_alert_count varchar [note: 'Total number of alerts that have been suppressed by this rule since creation.']
  suppression_duration_minutes varchar [note: 'Duration in minutes for which alerts will be suppressed once the rule is triggered.']
  tags varchar [note: 'Comma-separated list of tags or labels for categorizing and filtering suppression rules.']
  target_environment varchar [note: 'Infrastructure environment where the suppression rule is enforced, such as production or staging.']
  target_region_code varchar [note: 'Geographic region or datacenter location code where the suppression rule applies.']
  target_service_name varchar [note: 'Name of the specific game service or infrastructure component to which this suppression rule applies.']
  threshold_unit varchar [note: 'Unit of measurement for the threshold value, such as percentage, count, milliseconds, or concurrent users.']
  threshold_value decimal(18,4) [note: 'Numeric threshold value used in condition evaluation for threshold-based suppression rules.']
  timezone varchar [note: 'Timezone identifier for interpreting time-based suppression windows, following IANA timezone database format.']
  version_number varchar [note: 'Version number of this suppression rule configuration, incremented with each modification for change tracking.']

  Note: 'Master reference table for alert_suppression_rule. Referenced by suppression_rule_id.'
}

Table quality.defect {
  defect_id varchar [pk, note: 'Unique surrogate identifier for each defect record in the quality management system. Primary key for the defect data product. TRANSACTION_HEADER role — entity represents a discrete QA lifecycle event with its own identity, workflow, and triage history.']
  ab_experiment_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to analytics.ab_experiment. Business justification: Defects discovered during A/B tests need experiment context for impact assessment; determines if bug affects experiment validity or is variant-specific, critical for experiment integrity.']
  asset_bundle_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to content.asset_bundle. Business justification: Defects in bundle packaging, compression, encryption, or CDN distribution (bundle fails to download, decompression error, missing assets in bundle). Bundle-level QA and deployment issue tracking.']
  asset_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to content.asset. Business justification: Defects frequently reference specific assets causing bugs (broken texture, corrupted audio, malformed model). QA workflow links defect reports to asset IDs for root cause analysis and artist assignmen']
  asset_version_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to content.asset_version. Business justification: Defects tied to specific asset revisions for regression tracking (\'Bug introduced in asset v1.23, fixed in v1.25\'). Version-level defect tracking enables precise rollback and bisection.']
  assignee_employee_id varchar [note: 'Reference to the developer or QA engineer currently assigned to investigate or resolve this defect. Maps to Jira \'Assignee\' field. Used for workload balancing and fix accountability tracking.']
  battle_pass_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to monetization.battle_pass. Business justification: Battle pass bugs (progression tracking failures, reward delivery errors, tier unlock issues, premium upgrade problems) must reference the specific battle pass instance being tested to isolate configur']
  build_artifact_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to title.build_artifact. Business justification: Defect tracking systems must link bugs to specific build artifacts for regression analysis, fix verification, and release gate decisions. Replaces string affected_build_version with proper FK for buil']
  build_submission_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to platform.build_submission. Business justification: Defects discovered in specific build submissions during cert review; QA tracks which submission introduced or fixed each bug for regression analysis.']
  certification_checklist_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to platform.certification_checklist. Business justification: Defects often reference specific TRC/TCR requirement violations; QA links bugs to checklist items for cert remediation tracking and waiver requests.']
  compliance_incident_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to compliance.compliance_incident. Business justification: Defects can escalate to compliance incidents (data leak bug, age-gate bypass, privacy violation). Real business process: incident escalation and regulatory notification.']
  content_patch_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to content.patch. Business justification: Defects fixed in specific patches for patch defect tracking (\'Fixed in patch 1.2.3\'). Patch-scoped defect management enables patch notes generation and regression testing.']
  defect_employee_id varchar [note: 'Reference to the workforce member (QA tester, playtester, UAT participant, or live ops analyst) who originally reported this defect. Supports reporter workload analysis and QA team performance metrics.']
  dev_project_id varchar [note: 'Reference to the Jira project or game development project under which this defect was filed. Supports project-level quality metrics, sprint velocity, and release gate tracking.']
  dlc_bundle_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to title.dlc_bundle. Business justification: DLC-specific defects (content bugs, entitlement issues) require isolation for hotfix prioritization and DLC certification workflows. Critical for post-launch content quality tracking and DLC-specific ']
  drm_entitlement_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to platform.drm_entitlement. Business justification: Defects in DRM systems (license validation failures, offline play bugs) linked to entitlement configurations for debugging and cert compliance.']
  gacha_pool_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to monetization.gacha_pool. Business justification: Gacha system defects (incorrect drop rates, pity counter failures, featured item boost errors, UI display bugs) must link to the specific pool configuration under test for compliance validation and de']
  game_server_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to infrastructure.game_server. Business justification: Defects discovered on specific game servers during live ops or QA testing require server context (build version, configuration, environment) for reproduction and root cause analysis. Essential for def']
  game_title_id varchar [note: 'Reference to the game title affected by this defect. Enables cross-title defect analysis, per-title quality benchmarking, and release readiness reporting. Serves as the PARTY_REFERENCE (resource reference) for this transaction.']
  iap_transaction_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to monetization.iap_transaction. Business justification: Defects discovered during IAP purchase flows (payment failures, UI bugs, entitlement grant errors) must link to the specific transaction that exposed the issue for root cause analysis and monetization']
  ip_agreement_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to licensing.ip_agreement. Business justification: Defects can arise from licensed middleware bugs, third-party SDK limitations, or content restrictions in IP agreements. QA teams track which defects require licensor notification or approval for worka']
  level_map_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to content.level_map. Business justification: Defects in level geometry, navmesh holes, spawn point issues, objective marker bugs, or level-specific performance problems. Level-scoped defect tracking for environment artist and level designer assi']
  localization_string_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to content.localization_string. Business justification: Defects for text bugs (string truncation, encoding error, missing variable substitution, rich text formatting broken). Localization QA workflow links defects to specific string keys for translator rem']
  ml_model_registry_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to analytics.ml_model_registry. Business justification: ML models predict defect severity, priority, or fix time; linking enables model validation and retraining with actual defect outcomes for improved triage automation.']
  network_region_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to infrastructure.network_region. Business justification: Region-specific defects (latency issues, geo-restricted content bugs, compliance violations) require region context. QA teams prioritize fixes based on regional player population, revenue impact, and ']
  npc_definition_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to content.npc_definition. Business justification: Defects in NPC behavior, animation, dialogue, loot tables, or spawn rules (broken AI pathfinding, missing dialogue, incorrect loot drop). NPC-scoped defect tracking for AI designer and content creator']
  platform_cert_submission_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to platform.cert_submission. Business justification: Defects discovered during certification review linked to specific submissions; tracking enables submission outcome analysis and resubmission planning.']
  platform_sku_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to platform.platform_sku. Business justification: Defects often SKU-specific (Deluxe Edition DLC bugs, regional pricing issues); QA tracks bugs per SKU for release planning and edition testing.']
  player_behavior_segment_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to analytics.player_behavior_segment. Business justification: Defects affecting specific player segments (whales, new players) need segment context for prioritization and impact analysis; high-value segment bugs are prioritized higher.']
  regulatory_obligation_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to compliance.regulatory_obligation. Business justification: Defects flagged as compliance-blocking (COPPA violation, age-gate failure, privacy breach) link to specific regulatory obligations. Real business process: compliance impact triage and remediation trac']
  reporter_player_account_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to player.player_account. Business justification: Player-reported bugs (via in-game reporting tools, community forums, support tickets) must track the reporting player for follow-up communication, bug bounty/reputation systems (rewarding quality repo']
  seasonal_event_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to content.seasonal_event. Business justification: Defects in event mechanics, rewards, eligibility rules, or content (broken event quest, missing reward, incorrect participation tracking). Event-scoped defect tracking for live ops issue management.']
  server_fleet_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to infrastructure.server_fleet. Business justification: Fleet-wide defects (performance degradation, configuration issues affecting all instances) require fleet-level tracking. QA teams assess impact scope and prioritize fixes based on fleet CCU and revenu']
  sprint_id varchar [note: 'Reference to the Jira sprint in which this defect is currently assigned or was resolved. Enables sprint-level defect density and velocity analysis.']
  storefront_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to platform.storefront. Business justification: Defects in storefront integration (IAP failures, listing bugs) linked to specific storefronts for resolution and SDK debugging.']
  test_case_id varchar [note: 'Reference to the QA test case that first identified this defect. Enables traceability from defect back to test coverage and supports test effectiveness analysis.']
  tournament_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to esports.tournament. Business justification: Tournament builds require dedicated QA cycles; defects found during tournament-specific testing (patch validation, LAN server stability) must reference the tournament for release gate decisions and co']
  affected_component varchar [note: 'The specific game system, module, or subsystem where the defect manifests (e.g., rendering engine, physics, UI/HUD, matchmaking, in-game store, audio, save system, network layer, NPC AI). Maps to the Jira \'Component\' field.']
  affected_platform varchar [note: 'The target platform(s) on which the defect is reproducible (e.g., PC, PlayStation 5, Xbox Series X, Nintendo Switch, iOS, Android). Critical for platform certification readiness and TRC/TCR compliance tracking. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: PC|PS5|PS4|XSX|XB1|Switch|iOS|Android|Stadia|VR — promote to reference product]']
  closed_timestamp timestamp [note: 'The date and time when the defect was formally closed after verification testing confirmed the fix. Distinct from resolved_timestamp — closure requires QA sign-off. Used in defect lifecycle duration analysis.']
  compliance_impact varchar [note: 'Identifies whether this defect has a regulatory or platform certification compliance impact. None = no compliance concern; ESRB/PEGI/USK/CERO/GRAC = content rating violation; PCI DSS = payment security issue; GDPR/COPPA = player data privacy violation; TRC/TCR = platform technical certification requirement failure. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: none|esrb|pegi|usk|cero|grac|pci_dss|gdpr|coppa|trc|tcr|ftc — promote to reference product]']
  created_timestamp timestamp [note: 'The date and time when this defect record was first ingested into the Silver layer data product. Serves as the RECORD_AUDIT_CREATED field for data lineage and ETL audit purposes. Distinct from reported_timestamp which reflects the business event time.']
  defect_description varchar [note: 'Full narrative description of the defect including observed behavior, expected behavior, and any additional context provided by the reporter. Sourced from the Jira issue description field.']
  defect_status varchar [note: 'Current workflow lifecycle state of the defect. Drives triage, assignment, and release gate decisions. Maps to Jira issue status. Serves as the LIFECYCLE_STATUS for this transaction. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: open|in_progress|resolved|closed|reopened|deferred|wont_fix|duplicate — promote to reference product]']
  defect_type varchar [note: 'Classification of the defect by its nature. Functional = incorrect game logic; Performance = FPS drops, load time, memory; Visual = rendering/LOD/UI artifact; Crash = application termination; Regression = previously fixed issue reintroduced; Localization = text/audio translation issue; Compliance = ESRB/PEGI/platform TRC violation; Security = vulnerability or exploit. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: functional|performance|visual|crash|regression|localization|compliance|security|audio|network — promote to reference product]']
  discovery_phase varchar [note: 'The development or operational phase during which the defect was discovered. Drives cost-of-quality analysis — defects found in live operations are significantly more expensive to remediate than those found in QA. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: qa|playtesting|uat|live_ops|certification|alpha|beta|pre_production — promote to reference product]']
  environment varchar [note: 'The environment in which the defect was identified and reproduced. Determines the urgency and escalation path — production defects require immediate live ops response.']
  escalation_reason varchar [note: 'Free-text explanation of why this defect was escalated. Populated only when is_escalated is True. Provides context for executive review and post-mortem analysis.']
  fix_version varchar [note: 'The planned or actual game build version in which this defect is targeted to be resolved. Set during triage and updated as sprint planning evolves. Maps to Jira \'Fix Version/s\' field.']
  has_waiver boolean [note: 'Indicates whether a formal risk-acceptance waiver has been granted for this defect, allowing the game to ship with the known issue. True = waiver approved. Critical for release governance and post-launch accountability.']
  is_certification_blocker boolean [note: 'Indicates whether this defect will block platform certification submission (e.g., fails a TRC or TCR requirement for PlayStation, Xbox, or Nintendo). True = certification cannot proceed until resolved. Critical for release gate management.']
  is_escalated boolean [note: 'Indicates whether this defect has been escalated beyond the standard triage process, typically due to severity, compliance impact, or executive visibility. True = escalated. Triggers additional reporting and stakeholder notification workflows.']
  is_regression boolean [note: 'Indicates whether this defect is a regression — a previously fixed issue that has reappeared in a newer build. True = confirmed regression. Used to track regression rate as a key release quality KPI and to flag CI/CD pipeline failures.']
  jira_issue_key varchar [note: 'The externally-known Jira issue key (e.g., GAME-1234) that uniquely identifies this defect in the source system of record. Used for cross-referencing with Jira dashboards, sprint boards, and release planning. Serves as the BUSINESS_IDENTIFIER for this transaction.']
  linked_jira_issue_keys varchar [note: 'Comma-separated list of related Jira issue keys (e.g., duplicate of, blocks, is blocked by, relates to). Preserves the Jira issue link graph in denormalized form for Silver layer analytics without requiring a separate junction table at this stage.']
  priority varchar [note: 'Business urgency level indicating how quickly the defect must be addressed relative to release schedules and player impact. Blocker = must fix before release; High = fix in current sprint; Medium = fix in near-term sprint; Low = fix when capacity allows. Distinct from severity — a cosmetic defect on the main menu may be low severity but high priority.']
  regression_introduced_version varchar [note: 'The build version in which the regression was reintroduced, if is_regression is True. Used to identify the offending code change or asset update via Perforce Helix Core history. Null if not a regression.']
  reported_timestamp timestamp [note: 'The date and time when the defect was first reported and logged in Jira. Serves as the BUSINESS_EVENT_TIMESTAMP for this transaction. Used to calculate defect age, SLA compliance, and time-to-triage metrics.']
  reproducibility varchar [note: 'Indicates how consistently the defect can be reproduced following the documented reproduction steps. Always = 100% reproducible; Intermittent = occurs sometimes; Rare = very infrequent; Unable to Reproduce = cannot be replicated. Affects triage priority and fix confidence.']
  reproduction_steps varchar [note: 'Step-by-step instructions required to reliably reproduce the defect. Critical for developers to diagnose and fix the issue. Sourced from the Jira \'Steps to Reproduce\' field.']
  resolved_timestamp timestamp [note: 'The date and time when the defect was marked as resolved or fixed in Jira. Used to calculate time-to-fix, fix cycle time, and release readiness metrics. Null if the defect is not yet resolved.']
  root_cause_category varchar [note: 'High-level classification of the underlying cause of the defect used for trend analysis and process improvement. Examples: code logic error, asset pipeline issue, configuration error, third-party SDK defect, design specification gap, environment mismatch. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: code_logic|asset_pipeline|configuration|third_party_sdk|design_spec|environment|data_corruption|race_condition — promote to reference product]']
  severity varchar [note: 'Technical impact level of the defect on game functionality or player experience. Critical = game crash or data loss; Major = significant feature broken; Minor = partial feature degradation; Trivial = cosmetic or negligible impact. Assigned during triage and may be updated post-triage.']
  source_system varchar [note: 'The operational system of record from which this defect record originated. Jira is the primary source; Zendesk player support tickets, GameAnalytics crash reports, or manual playtesting logs may also feed defects. Used for data lineage and ETL audit.']
  title varchar [note: 'Short, human-readable summary of the defect as entered in Jira. Used in dashboards, triage meetings, and release notes to identify the issue at a glance.']
  triage_date date [note: 'The date on which the defect was formally triaged by the QA lead and development team. Captures the most recent triage event date. Used to measure time-to-triage SLA compliance.']
  triage_outcome varchar [note: 'The formal decision reached during the most recent triage session. Accepted = will be fixed; Deferred = moved to a future release; Rejected = not a valid defect; Duplicate = already tracked under another issue; Won\'t Fix = known issue accepted as-is.']
  triage_participants varchar [note: 'Comma-separated list of workforce member names or IDs who participated in the triage session. Provides accountability and audit trail for triage decisions. Stored as a denormalized string for Silver layer simplicity.']
  updated_timestamp timestamp [note: 'The date and time when this defect record was last modified in the data product. Serves as the RECORD_AUDIT_UPDATED field. Used to detect stale records and track synchronization lag from Jira.']
  waiver_approver varchar [note: 'Name or identifier of the authorized individual (e.g., QA Director, Studio Head, Legal) who approved the non-certification waiver. Provides accountability for risk-acceptance decisions.']
  waiver_expiry_date date [note: 'The date on which the non-certification waiver expires and must be reviewed or renewed. Ensures waivers do not persist indefinitely and are re-evaluated at each release cycle. Null if no waiver exists.']
  waiver_justification varchar [note: 'Formal written justification for the risk-acceptance waiver, documenting why the defect is acceptable to ship. Required for audit and compliance purposes. Populated only when has_waiver is True.']

  Note: 'Master record for all bugs, defects, and issues identified during QA, playtesting, UAT, or live operations. Tracks severity, priority, reproduction steps, affected build/platform/component, assignee, status lifecycle, root cause classification, regression flag, and ESRB/PEGI compliance impact. Includes full triage event history (triage date, outcome, participants, assigned severity/priority post-triage, target fix version, escalation flag) and non-certification waiver tracking for known defects shipped with risk acceptance (justification, approver, expiry). Sourced from Jira. SSOT for all defect records, their triage decisions, and risk-acceptance disposition across all game titles and platforms.'
}

Table quality.test_case {
  test_case_id varchar [pk, note: 'Unique surrogate identifier for each test case record in the QA test case library. Primary key for the test_case data product.']
  asset_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to content.asset. Business justification: Test cases validate specific assets (e.g., \'Verify hero_model.fbx loads in <2s\', \'Validate weapon_fire.wav plays correctly\'). Asset-specific test coverage tracking for compliance and performance valid']
  battle_pass_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to monetization.battle_pass. Business justification: Battle pass features require dedicated test cases for progression tracking, XP accumulation, tier unlocks, reward delivery, premium upgrade flows, and cross-platform sync—all scoped to specific battle']
  certification_checklist_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to platform.certification_checklist. Business justification: Test cases map to specific TRC/TCR requirements; test libraries organized by checklist version for cert coverage analysis and gap identification.']
  dlc_bundle_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to title.dlc_bundle. Business justification: DLC certification requires dedicated test suites for expansion content, entitlements, and base game integration. DLC-level test case organization is required for platform holder submissions and DLC-sp']
  drm_entitlement_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to platform.drm_entitlement. Business justification: Test cases validate DRM entitlement scenarios (family sharing, device binding); required for platform cert compliance and TRC validation.']
  game_studio_id varchar [note: 'Reference to the development studio responsible for the game component being tested. Supports studio-level quality benchmarking and QA resource allocation reporting.']
  game_title_id varchar [note: 'Reference to the game title that this test case is associated with. Enables game-level quality reporting, cross-title test coverage analysis, and release readiness dashboards per game IP.']
  ip_agreement_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to licensing.ip_agreement. Business justification: Test cases verify compliance with licensed IP usage terms (content restrictions, trademark guidelines, middleware integration requirements). QA teams design test suites to validate agreement obligatio']
  level_map_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to content.level_map. Business justification: Test cases validate specific levels (collision detection, lighting quality, spawn point functionality, objective marker visibility, navmesh coverage). Level-specific test coverage for environment vali']
  localization_string_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to content.localization_string. Business justification: Test cases validate specific strings (character limit compliance, variable substitution correctness, rich text rendering, gender/plural variants). String-level test coverage for localization quality a']
  mtx_catalog_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to monetization.mtx_catalog. Business justification: Test cases validate specific MTX items (pricing display accuracy, purchase flow completion, entitlement grant, platform fee calculation). Each test case targets specific catalog SKUs for certification']
  npc_definition_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to content.npc_definition. Business justification: Test cases validate NPC AI behavior, spawn rules, combat mechanics, dialogue trees, loot tables. NPC-specific test coverage for gameplay systems validation and balance testing.']
  platform_sku_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to platform.platform_sku. Business justification: Test cases validate SKU-specific features (edition content, regional variants); test coverage tracked per SKU for certification completeness.']
  regulatory_obligation_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to compliance.regulatory_obligation. Business justification: Test cases validate specific regulatory requirements (ESRB content checks, GDPR consent flows, COPPA age gates). Real business process: compliance test planning and regulatory validation.']
  seasonal_event_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to content.seasonal_event. Business justification: Test cases validate event functionality, eligibility rules, reward distribution, recurrence logic. Event-specific test coverage for live ops quality assurance and player experience validation.']
  storefront_id varchar [note: 'Reference to the primary target platform for this test case (e.g., PS5, Xbox Series X, PC, iOS, Android). Enables platform-specific quality reporting and certification readiness tracking per platform.']
  test_suite_id varchar [note: 'Foreign key linking to quality.test_suite. Business justification: test_case currently has test_suite as STRING (denormalized). Normalizing by adding test_suite_id FK to test_suite master reference table. The test_suite STRING column becomes redundant as suite_name c']
  approved_by varchar [note: 'Name or username of the QA lead or manager who approved this test case for active use in the test library. Provides governance accountability for test case quality and completeness.']
